# Unofficial Meeting Notes
## Conroe ISD Audit and Investment Committee Meeting
### September 13, 2023 — Regular Meeting

> **Note:** These are **unofficial notes** based on an auto-generated transcript of the meeting. They may contain errors or omissions.

---

## Attendance & Quorum

A quorum was **not present** for this meeting. As a result, the committee could receive information on agenda items but was **unable to vote or take formal action** on Items 2 and 6. Item 2 (approval of prior meeting minutes) was skipped and will be addressed at the next meeting.

---

## Citizen Participation (Item 1)

No members of the public signed up to speak.

---

## Item 3: Status of 2022–2023 Internal Audit Plan and 2023–2024 Internal Audit Plan

### 2022–2023 Audit Plan (Complete)

**Activity Fund Audits:** All completed.

**Katy Creek Upgrade Construction Audit:**
- Backend audit reviewing numbers against invoices, subcontractor contracts, etc.
- CISD Planning & Construction was found to have **great controls** over their financials.
- **One observation noted:** 2 out of 37 subcontractor pay applications lacked detailed information sufficient for an outside auditor to determine what costs were for. P&C staff understood the items, but documentation was insufficient for independent review.
- Discussion about whether P&C can maintain this quality level if the district scales up with a potential new bond. The internal auditor suggested it would be beneficial to have an outside expert verify that materials ordered match what is delivered.

**PEIMS Attendance Audit:**
- Conducted annually to ensure attendance is taken accurately, as state revenue depends on it.
- **Methodology was refined this year:** Missing submissions were cross-checked to confirm the teacher was actually present that day (substitutes take manual attendance that doesn't register in the electronic system).
- **Sample:** 10 schools over 5 different dates.
- **Error definition:** Attendance taken more than one hour after the designated time (when students are no longer present) or truly missing submissions where the teacher was present.
- **Results:**
  - ~4,600 class instances reviewed
  - 413 errors (attendance taken outside the one-hour window)
  - 214 actual missing submissions (teacher present, attendance not taken)
  - **Overall error rate: 13.5%** — an improvement from the prior year
- **High schools had higher error rates** than elementary and junior high, attributed to the fact that high school teachers must take attendance multiple times per day (4–7 periods), making it more burdensome.
- Some high schools now use a special bell at the official attendance time as a reminder.
- Automated solutions (e.g., smart ID tags/scanners) were briefly discussed but deemed cost-prohibitive and potentially problematic (tracking concerns, lost cards, etc.).
- Dr. Null requested that this data be shared with assistant superintendents and principals, noting that principals are competitive and seeing campus-level comparisons could help motivate improvement.

### 2023–2024 Audit Plan (In Progress)

Audits planned for the current year:
1. **Activity Funds** — one already completed
2. **Payroll Audit** — in final week of field work; focused on process, controls, segregation of duties, and flow-charting all processes for future reference. Last payroll audit was 3 years ago. A new payroll director (Chase) is also in place.
3. **Accounts Payable** — including a review of the **travel card program**
4. **Cybersecurity Risk Assessment** — Weaver engagement; kickoff meeting scheduled for **January 11, 2024**, with field work starting **January 15, 2024**
5. **Field Trip expenditures** (~$1 million/year)
6. **Hometown Athletic Ticketing System** — will have been in use for a year
7. **Child Nutrition follow-up**
8. **Band Inventory** — high schools in May, all others in November
9. **Maintenance follow-up**
10. **Annual PEIMS attendance review**
11. **Construction Audit**
12. **Print Shop** (lowest priority; will be completed if time permits)

**Notable Discussion — Travel Cards:**
- The district historically was "very anti-credit card" and had only 3 district credit cards for emergencies.
- As hotels increasingly refuse checks, the district implemented **pre-loaded travel cards** with merchant category code restrictions (cards only work at the designated hotel).
- Cards are checked out by employees, loaded with the specific PO amount, and returned with receipts after travel.
- Cards can be adjusted remotely in real time if needed and deactivated immediately if lost.
- The district has approximately 100 cards stored but they carry zero balances until activated for specific travel.
- The program started with student travel in spring 2023 and expanded to all employee travel as of September 1, 2023.
- **The district does not use P-cards (purchasing cards).** Maintenance and other departments use internal purchase orders for procurement.

---

## Item 4: Potential Independent Program Analysis of Planning & Construction

Dr. Null presented a proposal to engage an **independent industry expert** to evaluate the Planning & Construction Department's **front-end processes** ahead of the potential new bond program (~$1.6–$1.8 billion in construction work).

**Background:**
- Community members during the 2019 bond cycle asked how the district ensures it gets the best prices.
- Previous backend audits have confirmed the district gets what it pays for; this initiative focuses on the **front end** — procurement, guaranteed maximum pricing, contracting, and organizational structure.

**Proposed Scope:**
- Organizational structure and staffing adequacy
- Procurement methods and bidding processes
- Interview/selection processes for contractors and architects
- Oversight of contractors and architects
- Project management practices
- Construction standards
- Compliance with applicable law
- Financial processes on the back end

**Key Details:**
- Kyle Holland, President/CEO of the Associated General Contractors of America (Houston Chapter), helped identify a potential consultant — a former school district P&C leader (~15 years experience) now working as a consultant evaluating such programs.
- The consultant would **report directly to the Audit Committee** to maintain independence.
- No contract has been signed yet; the committee was asked for feedback.
- Mr. Foster (head of P&C) is reportedly supportive and sees it as an opportunity for improvement.

**Committee Discussion:**
- Trustee Chase emphasized interest in the exact scope of work, the consultant's evaluation expertise, and how "best value" is defined (not just cheapest price, but also risk transfer).
- Noted the district already uses **Construction Manager at Risk (CMAR)** as its default construction method.
- Raised concern about **Key Personnel Risk** — the district is lean, and much institutional knowledge exists only in individuals' heads, which creates risk, especially with scaling up.
- Dr. Null committed to sharing the proposed scope of work with Trustee Chase for review.

**No vote taken** (informational item).

---

## Item 5: Investment Review and Results Update

Presented by Cara (Finance staff).

### Investment Strategy
- Operating funds invested in: State pools, WoodForest National Bank, commercial paper, and short-term government securities (≤1 year maturity)
- $50 million of unassigned fund balance managed by **TCG Investment Advisors** in a **36-month investment ladder**

### General Fund Portfolio Composition (as of 8/31/2023)
| Category | Amount | % of Portfolio |
|---|---|---|
| Agency Securities | $16.6M | 8.91% |
| Money Market (WoodForest) | $76.3M | (significant increase due to new depository contract: 90-day T-Bill + 25 basis points) |
| Municipal Bonds | $5.1M | 2.74% |
| State Pools | $56.5M | 30.25% |
| Treasury Securities | $32.2M | 17.25% |
| **Total General Fund Par Value** | **$186.99M** | |

### Interest Revenue (5-Year Trend)
- FY 2022: $1.47 million
- FY 2023: **$11.4 million** (significant increase due to rising interest rates)

### Combined Portfolio (All Funds, as of 8/31/2023)
- **Total par value: $433.92 million**
- Largest holdings: State pools ($32.9M noted, though this may reflect a transcript discrepancy with the general fund figure), Treasury securities ($32.2M), agencies ($16.6M), money market ($76.5M), municipal bonds ($5.1M)
- **Combined portfolio yield: 5.11%**
- **Benchmark (90-day T-Bill): 5.29%**

### Benchmark Analysis
- The portfolio has been playing "catch-up" to the 90-day T-Bill benchmark following 11 Fed rate increases since March 2022.
- State pools take approximately 60–90 days to adjust to rate increases.
- The gap between portfolio yield and benchmark has been narrowing.

  2. List of **designated investment officers**
  3. **Sources of instruction** for required 8 hours of investment training every 2 years
